coredata.dc:title: Ultraviolet light-driven controllable doping of graphene quantum dots with tunable emission wavelength for fluorescence bio-imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zihao
Liu, Zhiduo
Gu, Bingli
Gao, Bo
Wang, Ting
Zheng, Xiaohu
Wang, Gang
Guo, Qinglei
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: As an important approach for tuning the emission wavelength of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), controllable doping has attracted a great deal of attentions. Here, we reported an ultraviolet light-driven doping approach for GQDs at room temperature. N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are obtained via the reaction in dimethylformamide under the ultraviolet radiation. The emission wavelength can be tuned from 566 to 463 nm by controlling the doping concentration. The quantum yield of N-GQDs is demonstrated to be much higher than that of most reported GQDs and increases with the increase of the doping concentration. Finally, the synthesized N-GQDs are explored for the application in fluorescence bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Lattice doping
Biomaterials
Bio-imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) GQDs and (b) N-GQDs. Insets of (a) and (b) show the lateral size distribution histogram of GQDs and N-GQDs. AFM images of (c) GQDs and (d) N-GQDs. (e) HR-TEM image. The inset shows the FFT image of N-GQDs. (f) Raman spectrum of N-GQDs.

(a) XPS spectrum (b) core level C 1s XPS spectrum and (c) N 1s XPS spectrum of N-GQDs. (d) Normalized Uv–vis spectrum, PLE and PL spectra of N-GQDs. (e) PL decay curve of N-GQDs. (f) The PL intensity of N-GQDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg mL−1) varying with time under an ultraviolet radiation (250 W with a wavelength of 320 nm).

(a) Metabolic activity of fibroblast cells treated with different concentrations of N-GQDs (N-GQDs-2, N-GQDs-4, N-GQDs-6, N-GQDs-8 and N-GQDs-10). (b) Confocal fluorescence microphotograph of fibroblast cells incubated with 100 μg/mL N-GQDs.
PL performances of N-GQDs obtained under different reaction times.

head.ce:title: Ultraviolet light-driven controllable doping of graphene quantum dots with tunable emission wavelength for fluorescence bio-imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an important approach for tuning the emission wavelength of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), controllable doping has attracted a great deal of attentions. Here, we reported an ultraviolet light-driven doping approach for GQDs at room temperature. N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are obtained via the reaction in dimethylformamide under the ultraviolet radiation. The emission wavelength can be tuned from 566 to 463 nm by controlling the doping concentration. The quantum yield of N-GQDs is demonstrated to be much higher than that of most reported GQDs and increases with the increase of the doping concentration. Finally, the synthesized N-GQDs are explored for the application in fluorescence bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted increasing attentions due to their outstanding optical properties, high photoluminescent (PL) stability, low cytotoxicity, and favorable biocompatibility [1,2], which provide significant opportunities in bioimaging, biosensing, light emitting diodes, biological labeling, solar cells, lithium ion batteries and photocatalysis [1,2]. Therefore, continuous efforts have been pursued to develop facile, effective, low-cost approaches to synthesize GQDs, including hydrothermal methods, acid oxidation, electrochemical exfoliation, arc-discharge, pyrolysis, and microwave treatment. These approaches can be divided into two types: top-down and bottom-up approaches [3–5]. Top-down approaches involve the exfoliation and cut of sp2-carbon materials by using strong oxidants (such as KClO3) and acids (such as H2SO4). The bottom-up approaches refer to both dehydrogenation and carbonization progresses, in which GQDs are synthesized from polycyclic aromatic compounds or other aromatic structures of molecules. Adeline et al. reported a one-pot approach for creating GQDs by hydrothermal method with a superior range of detection towards DNA [5]. However, the difficulty in removing the byproducts (such as inorganic salts and acids) and the harsh synthesis conditions limit the large-scale production of GQDs. Moreover, GQDs synthesized by traditional methods have low quantum yield (φ) and poor crystallinity that hinder their further applications [6]. Therefore, developing a mild, green, and highly efficient synthetic pathway for synthesizing GQDs is urgently needed.

Here, we reported an ultraviolet light-driven doping approach for GQDs at room temperature and atmospheric pressure. With the free radical doping progress of GQDs in dimethylformamide (DMF), the N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are obtained. The doping concentration is controlled to be 0.2, 0.4, 0.7, 0.8 and 1.1 at.% when the reaction time is 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 h, respectively. The obvious blue shift of emission wavelength (λem) is observed with the increase of doping concentration. Moreover, the quantum yield of N-GQDs can be controlled by adjusting the doping concentration. Finally, given the good stability and low biotoxicity, we also demonstrated the application value of multicolored GQDs in fluorescent bioimaging.

GQDs (10 mg, 98%, Xiwang Technology Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) were dissolved in 50 mL DMF (Aladdin Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China). The mixture was exposed to an ultraviolet source (wavelength: 185–254 nm, power: 250 W, Guoda Shanghai, China Co., Ltd.) for 6 h at room temperature. Then the obtained mixture was filtered by 0.22 µm Nylon filter membrane. After that, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min and the supernatant was added into 50 mL water and dialysis (100 Da) for 3 days. The yield of N-GQDs is 87 wt%, which was calculated according to Eq. (1):
(1)
Yield =
M
N - GQDs
/
M GQDs
where MN-GQDs and MGQDs are the mass of obtained N-GQDs and precursor (GQDs).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out on Hitachi H-8100. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured by PHI Quantera II system, Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption properties were characterized by UV-5800 spectrophotometer. PL and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were collected from a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.). Raman spectra (HORIBA Jobin Yvon HR800) were measured using an Ar+ laser. Atomic Force Microscope (Cypher S, Asylum research) was used to determine the height profiles of N-GQDs.

Fig. 1 (a, b) shows the TEM images of GQDs and N-GQDs. An average size of 3.5 nm of both GQDs and N-GQDs are observed, as shown in Fig. 1(a, b) inserts. No obvious size difference can be found between GQDs and N-GQDs. Atomic force microscope (AFM) observations in Fig. 1(c, d) also reveal the ca. 1–3 layers structure of highly dispersed GQDs and N-GQDs on SiO2 substrate. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (Fig. 1(e)) of N-GQDs shows a 0.24 nm lattice spacing in crystalline structure, which is assigned as the (1 1 0) diffraction plane of graphene. Fast Fourier transform (FFT, Fig. 1(e) inset) of the TEM image of N-GQDs exhibits a standard six-fold symmetry, implying a good crystalline quality of N-GQDs. The crystalline quality of N-GQDs is demonstrated by Raman spectrum (Fig. 1(f)). The intensity ratio of the D (1337–1346 cm−1) band to G (1584–1589 cm−1) band (ID/IG), is calculated to be about 0.62. The relatively low value confirms the well crystallized sp2 structure of as-prepared N-GQDs [7,8].

XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 (a)) of N-GQDs shows a C 1s peak at 285.0 eV, O 1s peak at 531.7 eV and N 1s peak at 400.0 eV. This demonstrates the successful doping of N in GQDs, with a doping concentration of 0.7 at.%. Core level XPS spectra (C 1s and N 1 s) of N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 2(b, c). The peaks locating at 284.94, 285.22, 285.81, 286.27, 288.32 and 289.15 eV (Fig. 2(b)), are assigned as the C–C/CC, C–N/CN, C–OH, C–O–C, CO and COOH groups, respectively. The peaks locating at 399.28, 399.98 and 401.91 eV are generated from the C–N, CN, and aromatic N, respectively (Fig. 2(c)). These results demonstrate that by utilizing the proposed approach, successful doping of GQDs is realized, which is facilitated by the free radical doping progress between GQDs and highly reactive N containing free radicals.

Optical properties of N-GQDs are displayed in Fig. 2(d–f). The Uv–Vis spectrum of N-GQDs aqueous solution is shown in Fig. 2(d). Peaks locating at 210 nm and 440 nm are attributed to the π-π* transition (sp2 domains in N-GQDs) and n-π* transition (between N and sp2 carbon atoms) process in N-GQDs, respectively. The optical excitation wavelength (λex) of the N-GQDs is 466 nm, as proved by the PLE spectrum (Fig. 2(d)). The λem is centered at 522 nm which indicates the N-GQDs emit green PL. Compared with the precursor (GQDs), the λem of N-GQDs is blue shifted (the λex and λem of GQDs are 521 and 585, respectively), resulting from the negative inductive effect of N atoms. Moreover, the φ of GQDs also increases after N-doping. The φ of N-GQDs is 0.45, which is higher than that of the mostly reported GQDs [6]. This can be due to the n-π* transition process in N-GQDs. The PL decay curve of N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 2(e), where the PL lifetime is found to be 1.8 ns. The obtained N-GQDs also show excellent photostability (Fig. 2(f)). The PL intensity decreases less than 11% after 72 h ultraviolet radiation (250 W mercury lamp with a center wavelength of 320 nm).

Above results have demonstrated the successful doping and PL performance improvement of GQDs. Moreover, N-GQDs with controllable doping concentration is obtained by control of reaction time. As shown in Table 1 , the doping concentration is 0.2, 0.4, 0.7, 0.8 and 1.1 at.% when the reaction time is 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 h. Obvious blue shifted of λem is also demonstrated with the increased doping concentration. Moreover, the φ of N-GQDs can be also enhanced by increasing the doping concentration.

The potential application in fluorescence bio-imaging of these N-GQDs is further explored. Bio-compatibility of N-GQDs (N-GQDs-2, N-GQDs-4, N-GQDs-6, N-GQDs-8 and N-GQDs-10) is investigated by probing the metabolic activity of fibroblast cells. Fibroblast cells are cultured in 96-well plates with various concentrations of N-GQDs, and then incubate for 48 h (Fig. 3 (a)). Subsequently, a standard assay is performed to assess the cell viabilities. No significant reduction in cell viability is observed, indicating all these N-GQDs produced are completely bio-compatible and nontoxic in vitro. To assess the prospect of N-GQDs as a practical fluorescence bio-imaging material, N-GQDs internalized fibroblast cells are characterized by the confocal fluorescence microscope. Strong PL is observed inside the cells (Fig. 3(b)), suggesting that the N-GQDs have been successfully internalized by the fibroblast cells and could be used as an ideal bio-imaging material.

In summary, an effective and controllable doping approach of GQDs has been developed. N-GQDs are obtained via the reaction between dimethylformamide and GQDs under ultraviolet radiation at room temperature and atmospheric pressure. Controllable doping concentration in N-GQDs is realized by tuning the reaction time. With increased doping concentration, the emission wavelength of N-GQDs can be tuned from 566 to 463 nm. Moreover, the quantum yield of N-GQDs, increasing with the doping concentration, is much higher than that of the mostly reported GQDs. This work may pave the way for developing effective doping methods of GQDs, which offer numerous opportunities for fluorescence bio-imaging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants Nos. 11604167, 11704204 and 61604084), Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang province, China (Grant No. LY19A040004), Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (No. 2018A610093), K.C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

Glenn JS, Zhi XH, Ye W, Dezhi K, Shaozhuan H, Bo L, Hui YY. FlatChem 9 (2018) 8-14.
Adeline HL, Zdenek S, Daniel B, Pavel U, Alessandra B, Martin P. ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 8 (2016) 1951-1957.
Song Y, Zhu C, Song J, Li H, Du D. Lin Y. ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 9 (2017) 7399-7405.
Hwee LP, Petr S, Zdenek S, Ivo T, Martin P. J. Mater. Chem. A 1 (2013) 13146-13153.
Lu W, Zdenek S, Jan L, Martin P. J. Mater. Chem. C 2 (2014) 2887-2893.
